text: The surgeon applies entromedial traction to the gallbladder, using a harmonic scalpel to separate the posterior peritoneum above the Rouviere's sulcus. The Teflon jaw of the harmonic is positioned towards the cystic pedicle. Entrolateral traction is then applied to open the anterior peritoneum at the level where the gallbladder body contacts the liver.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to create a posterior window by opening the peritoneum at the level of the roviat sulcus. The peritoneal dissection of the pedicle is performed, facilitating the formation of an anterior window. The peritoneum is separated from above downwards.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to create the Calot's triangle, identifying the cystic artery and the inferior surface of the liver. A window is formed between the artery and the liver, as well as between the artery and the duct. Titanium clips are applied to the artery: the first clip is placed proximally, the second three millimeters away, and the third six millimeters above the second. Similarly, clips are applied to the duct, with the same spacing. The harmonic scalpel is then used to cut between the second and third clips on both the artery and duct.

The surgeon separates the cystic duct followed by the cystic artery, using entromedial and entrolateral traction to create a clear areolar plane. The gallbladder is gradually separated from the liver, with the Teflon jaw positioned to prevent puncture. The surgical field is inspected, showing no bleeding or bile leak, and all clips are securely in place before completing the separation of the gallbladder from the liver.
The surgeon introduces a claw forceps to grasp and extract the gallbladder from the surgical field.

The telescope is transferred to the epigastric port, while the umbilical port is used to retract the appendix and transfer it to the right lateral port. The right lateral port holds the appendix, and the umbilical port utilizes the harmonic device. This setup allows the appendectomy to be performed using the same three ports.

The right anterior axillary line port, previously used for fundus traction in cholecystectomy, now holds the appendix. The umbilical port serves as a working port, with a Maryland instrument creating a window and coagulating the appendicular artery. Scissors are used through the umbilical port to dissect the appendicular artery.

There is no any additional port. Only thing is that surgeon has to move towards the left shoulder. During colicectomy, you are near to the left hip and during appendicectomy, you have to move towards the left shoulder and camera person will go near the head of the patient and telescope is in the epigastric port.

The surgeon lifts the appendix and ties a knot at its base using the umbilical port. The appendix is then cut with scissors, and the harmonic device is used to further dissect and coagulate the base, ensuring the mucosa is sealed to prevent leakage.
The surgeon removes the appendix through the umbilical port using laparoscopic techniques.
